pages.title: Welcome to Social Engineering Awareness
pages.subtitle: Learn how attackers manipulate people, not just systems, to gain access to information, accounts, and physical spaces.

pages.title: What is social engineering?
pages.text:
Social engineering is the use of manipulation, deception, or pressure to trick people into revealing confidential information or taking unsafe actions.

Instead of breaking into a system through technical means, attackers often try to exploit trust, fear, urgency, curiosity, or helpfulness.

This can happen through email, phone calls, text messages, fake websites, in-person conversations, or physical access attempts.

Attackers often target people first because people can be easier to influence than systems are to break.

pages.title: The 4 key stages of social engineering
pages.text:
Many social engineering attacks follow a predictable pattern. Understanding these stages can help you recognize suspicious behavior earlier.

StageWhat Happens
1. Investigation / ReconnaissanceAttackers research targets using sources like social media, company websites, and public information to find weak points, high-value employees, and internal language that can help build a believable story.
2. Manipulation / HookThe attacker makes contact through phishing, phone calls, text messages, or in-person interaction. They often use urgency, fear, authority, or excitement to lower the target’s guard.
3. Exploitation / Playing the HookOnce trust is established, the attacker pressures the victim to take action, such as clicking a malicious link, sharing credentials, approving an MFA request, or allowing physical access.
4. Execution / ExitThe attacker achieves the goal, such as stealing data, installing malware, or entering a restricted area, and then cuts contact while trying to leave little evidence behind.

The earlier you recognize the pattern, the easier it is to stop the attack before damage happens.

image.alt: The four key stages of a social engineering attack

pages.title: Why attackers target people
pages.text:
People are often the easiest path into an organization.

An attacker may not need advanced hacking tools if they can convince someone to take a risky action.

Unsafe ActionWhy It Helps the Attacker
Share a passwordGives direct account access
Click a malicious linkCan lead to credential theft or malware
Open an infected attachmentMay install harmful software
Approve a login requestLets the attacker into a real account
Hold open a secure doorCreates physical access to restricted areas

Attackers know that employees want to be helpful, responsive, and cooperative. Social engineering takes advantage of those normal behaviors.

pages.title: Common manipulation tactics
pages.text:
Social engineering often relies on emotional pressure or false trust.

TacticExample
Urgency“Do this right now.”
Authority“I’m from IT, HR, or management.”
Fear“Your account will be locked.”
Curiosity“Open this file to see the update.”
Helpfulness“Can you help me real quick?”

A message or person may sound believable while still being unsafe.

image.alt: Examples of urgency, authority, fear, and curiosity

pages.title: Did you know?
pages.text:
Social engineering remains a common and effective attack method.

  • 43% of small-business cyber attacks were listed as phishing / social engineering.
  • 41% of higher-education cybersecurity incidents and breaches were caused by social engineering attacks.
  • 79% of financial institutions said cybercriminals had become more sophisticated, using highly targeted social engineering attacks.

Attackers often do not need to break systems first — they try to influence people first.

Source: PurpleSec Cybersecurity Statistics

pages.title: Pretexting and fake authority
pages.text:
Pretexting happens when someone creates a believable story to gain your trust.

Examples include:

  • someone claiming to be from IT and asking for your password
  • a caller pretending to be a vendor needing account details
  • a person claiming they were told to pick up records or equipment

Always verify requests for sensitive information through official channels before taking action.

image.alt: Example of fake authority or pretexting

pages.title: Tailgating and physical access
pages.text:
Social engineering is not limited to email, phone calls, or fake messages.

Tailgating happens when an unauthorized person follows an employee into a restricted area without proper access.

This may happen when someone says they forgot their badge, are in a hurry, are carrying items, or claim they were told to come in.

Do not assume someone belongs in a secure area just because they act confident, familiar, or rushed.

DoDo Not
  • Ask visitors to use the proper check-in process
  • Verify access if someone does not have a visible badge
  • Politely direct the person to security or the front desk
  • Report suspicious attempts to enter restricted spaces
  • Hold secure doors open for unknown individuals
  • Assume someone is authorized because they look professional
  • Ignore badge, visitor, or escort procedures
  • Feel pressured to break policy just to avoid awkwardness

image.alt: Unauthorized person entering behind an employee

pages.title: Baiting and curiosity traps
pages.text:
Attackers may also use baiting to tempt someone into unsafe action.

Examples include:

  • a USB drive left in a parking lot or lobby
  • a file labeled “Payroll” or “Confidential”
  • an unexpected attachment that looks important
  • a free download or reward that requires login information

Curiosity is normal, but unknown files, devices, or downloads should never be trusted automatically.

pages.title: Real-life example video
pages.text:
This short video shows how social engineering can be used to gain unauthorized access by manipulating people rather than attacking systems directly.

As you watch, pay attention to how trust, urgency, and human interaction are used to bypass normal security expectations.

Social engineering attacks often succeed because someone appears believable, helpful, or urgent.

This video is provided for educational and awareness purposes only. Project Playbook is not affiliated with, endorsed by, or sponsored by CNN.

pages.title: Common mistakes people make
pages.text:
Common MistakeSafer Response
Acting too quickly because a request feels urgentPause and verify first
Trusting someone just because they sound officialConfirm identity through known channels
Opening unexpected files or linksCheck the source before interacting
Letting someone into a secure space without confirmationFollow badge and access procedures

pages.title: What to do when something feels off
pages.text:
If a request feels unusual, rushed, or manipulative:

  1. Pause before acting
  2. Verify the request through official channels
  3. Do not share passwords, MFA codes, or confidential data
  4. Do not grant access to secure spaces without authorization
  5. Report suspicious behavior using your organization’s process

It is always better to verify than to guess wrong.

Pause, verify, and report. A few extra seconds can prevent a much bigger problem.
